title: Longest Substring Without Repeating
code: if (last.TryGetValue(c, out int j) && j >= left)
left = j + 1;            // jump past the previous occurrence

frames.caption: Set up an empty window [left, right] and a map last[c] of each character's most recent index. Walk right across the string once.
frames.note: This is the LONGEST variable-window template. We grow `right` unconditionally and only adjust `left` when the new character collides with one already inside the window. The map stores the last index seen — not just membership — because that index is exactly where `left` must jump to.

frames.caption: right=0: c='a' is fine (never seen before). Window stays "a".
frames.note: The window invariant is the whole game: s[left..right] always holds DISTINCT characters. This char has either never appeared or last appeared before `left`, so adding it keeps every character unique — no shrink needed, the window just grows by one on the right.

frames.caption: Record last['a'] = 0. Window [0, 0] = "a", length 1. best = max(best, 1) = 1.
frames.note: After validity is restored we record the width right − left + 1. This is the LONGEST template: shrink (here, jump) while invalid, then read best once valid. Because both `left` and `right` only ever move forward and are each bounded by n, the total pointer movement is at most 2n — that amortized bound is why the whole scan is O(n) despite looking nested.

frames.caption: right=1: c='b' is fine (never seen before). Window stays "ab".
frames.note: The window invariant is the whole game: s[left..right] always holds DISTINCT characters. This char has either never appeared or last appeared before `left`, so adding it keeps every character unique — no shrink needed, the window just grows by one on the right.

frames.caption: Record last['b'] = 1. Window [0, 1] = "ab", length 2. best = max(best, 2) = 2.
frames.note: After validity is restored we record the width right − left + 1. This is the LONGEST template: shrink (here, jump) while invalid, then read best once valid. Because both `left` and `right` only ever move forward and are each bounded by n, the total pointer movement is at most 2n — that amortized bound is why the whole scan is O(n) despite looking nested.

frames.caption: right=2: c='c' is fine (never seen before). Window stays "abc".
frames.note: The window invariant is the whole game: s[left..right] always holds DISTINCT characters. This char has either never appeared or last appeared before `left`, so adding it keeps every character unique — no shrink needed, the window just grows by one on the right.

frames.caption: Record last['c'] = 2. Window [0, 2] = "abc", length 3. best = max(best, 3) = 3.
frames.note: After validity is restored we record the width right − left + 1. This is the LONGEST template: shrink (here, jump) while invalid, then read best once valid. Because both `left` and `right` only ever move forward and are each bounded by n, the total pointer movement is at most 2n — that amortized bound is why the whole scan is O(n) despite looking nested.

frames.caption: right=3: c='a' was last seen at index 0, and 0 >= left (0) — it is inside the window. Duplicate!
frames.note: A character already in the window means the run s[left..right] would repeat. Validity has been broken by exactly one collision, and the map tells us precisely where the offender sits, so we can fix it in a single move instead of crawling.

frames.caption: Jump left to 0 + 1 = 1, past the old 'a'. The window is now distinct again: "bca".
frames.note: The jump is the LC 3 trick: instead of moving `left` one char at a time until the duplicate falls out, we leap straight to j + 1. Crawling is also O(n) (left never moves backward), but the jump skips the dead steps. Crucially `left` only ever increases — never rewind it, or you could re-admit a repeat.

frames.caption: Record last['a'] = 3. Window [1, 3] = "bca", length 3. best = max(best, 3) = 3.
frames.note: After validity is restored we record the width right − left + 1. This is the LONGEST template: shrink (here, jump) while invalid, then read best once valid. Because both `left` and `right` only ever move forward and are each bounded by n, the total pointer movement is at most 2n — that amortized bound is why the whole scan is O(n) despite looking nested.

Because both `left` and `right` only ever move forward and are each bounded by n, the total pointer movement is at most 2n — that amortized bound is why the whole scan is O(n) despite looking nested.

frames.caption: End of string. The longest window without repeating characters has length 3 (e.g. "abc").
frames.note: Every index entered the window once (via `right`) and left at most once (via `left`), so the work totals O(n); the map costs O(min(n, alphabet)) space. The answer is the widest distinct window ever recorded — not the final window, which is why best is tracked as a running maximum.
